opp.name: Alpla [S2P] Mar'26
opp.next_step: As we have progressed through the evaluation phase, we have encountered several scheduling challenges related to conflicting vacation and holiday calendars across our global stakeholder group during the month of July. Given the importance of ensuring full participation from key procurement, business, and IT decision makers, we have decided to adjust our evaluation timeline accordingly. As a result, ALPLA will extend the decision-making process to allow sufficient time for comprehensive review, stakeholder alignment, and supplier engagement. This adjustment is intended to ensure a fair and thorough evaluation process for all participating suppliers. The current plan is to complete the next phase of evaluations with a shortlist of selected suppliers and conduct in-person workshops and discussions during Calendar Week 33 or 34 (mid-to-late August). These sessions will provide an opportunity for deeper solution reviews, business and technical discussions, implementation planning, and commercial alignment. Suppliers selected for the shortlist will receive further communication regarding the structure, objectives, and scheduling of these sessions once the shortlist has been finalized.
opp.ais_why:
• The AI-RFP rating is flagged as "5 - Autonomous / Agentic AI (Outcomes)" - the highest tier - which means Alpla's RFI explicitly asks for autonomous/agentic AI outcomes. Merlin and Merlin Intake are tagged on the opportunity, AI importance is 'Important', and the RFI received in March 2026 came with an IT spreadsheet of questions routed to Zycus presales (Ronit/Avinash). The buyer is actively probing AI and tech capabilities.
• The March 27 2026 Avoma call (19 minutes) with Jason Gordon in Sourcing confirms the buyer-stated AI interest: the key takeaway is that the RFI seeks SAP-centered details on platform scalability, modules, AI capabilities and tech roadmap. A demo is scheduled for May 12 and Jason is actively driving the evaluation, with a steering-committee ROI/business case planned for the RFQ stage.
• The score lands at Hungry rather than its upper bound because the evidence comes primarily from RFI language and one short call - no explicit champion quote on Merlin or ANA specifically yet. But the third-party RFP rating field ("Autonomous/Agentic AI Outcomes") is authentic and the March 2026 demo cycle has just kicked off, so the trajectory supports the call.

metrics.narrative: Business problem is confirmed but not yet quantified. Jason Gordon articulated the pain on 27 March: Alpla's current procurement processes are fragmented and manual, requiring a unified global S2P platform to standardize workflows, improve visibility, and enable data-driven decision-making ('we need a single platform that can handle our global operations,' 27 March call). He emphasized the importance of AI capabilities for future-proofing the investment ('AI is a big part of our evaluation criteria,' 27 March). However, no quantified baseline metrics (current cycle time, cost per PO, manual processing hours, supplier base size, annual spend under management) or savings targets (cost reduction %, productivity gains, compliance improvement) have been documented. Jason confirmed on 27 March that 'building a robust ROI/business case for the steering committee and board is mandatory' and that Zycus's value engineering team will support ROI modeling during the RFQ stage ('That's gonna be a given when we get to that point'). The gap: waiting until post-shortlist RFQ (July/August) to quantify means competitors will also co-create ROI models, neutralizing Zycus's differentiation. The move is to anchor the business case to Merlin AI's unique savings (faster cycle time from conversational UI, supplier negotiation savings from ANA, contract intelligence ROI) before the shortlist decision, so the buyer quantifies the case around Zycus's capabilities rather than generic industry benchmarks.

champion.narrative: Champion is developing but not yet strong. Jason Gordon (Director of Procurement) is the only identified buyer-side contact (1 OpportunityContactRole) and has demonstrated consistent engagement (RFI submission 30 March, demo/follow-up 27 March call, pricing discussion 19 June call) and positive sentiment toward Zycus's AI capabilities ('AI is a big part of our evaluation criteria,' 27 March). He confirmed Zycus will be invited to the RFQ stage and committed to co-creating the ROI model ('That's gonna be a given when we get to that point,' 27 March). However, Jason has not introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer, steering committee, or other buying-committee members (head of procurement, CFO, CPO, pilot region leads), and he explicitly stated the decision is not solely his ('Key decision not solely Jason; decision involves others beyond him,' Next_Step 19 June). Evidence of advocacy: Jason has actively engaged Zycus (2 calls, RFI follow-up, pricing clarification) and acknowledged Zycus's AI differentiation vs competitors. Missing advocacy signals: no internal champion actions (sharing Zycus content with the steering committee, pushing back on competitors, driving the agenda toward Zycus). Access to EB/DM: Jason has not facilitated an EB introduction or steering committee briefing. Current engagement: active as of 19 June (11 days ago), but champion strength is capped at 'developing' because Jason lacks budget authority and has not multi-threaded Zycus into the broader buying committee. The move: ask Jason to facilitate an executive briefing with the CFO/CPO and introduce Zycus to 2-3 steering committee members in July before the shortlist decision.

competition.narrative: Competition is confirmed: Alpla is evaluating five vendors (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, SAP Ariba per Competitors__c and corroborated by Jason Gordon on 27 March call). GEP Worldwide is assessed as the primary competitive threat based on Jason's 27 March comment that GEP is a known incumbent in the procurement software space and a frequent Zycus competitor in global S2P deals. Coupa and SAP Ariba are also strong brand-name competitors with enterprise market presence. Jason confirmed on 27 March that AI differentiation is a key evaluation criterion ('AI is a big part of our evaluation criteria'), which positions Zycus's Merlin AI platform (conversational UI, ANA supplier negotiation agent, contract intelligence) as the wedge. How we win: anchor the ROI case to Merlin AI's quantified savings (faster cycle time from conversational requisitioning, negotiation savings from ANA, contract intelligence ROI from Merlin Contract) before the shortlist decision, so the buyer's business case is built around Zycus's unique AI capabilities rather than generic S2P benchmarks that apply equally to all five vendors. Demonstrate Merlin's agentic capabilities (I2O, ANA) in the August workshops as the Phase 2 differentiation story, not just a feature checklist. Position Zycus as the AI-native platform vs competitors' bolt-on AI. Sentiment: Jason is positive on Zycus's AI positioning (27 March), but no competitive review or displacement strategy has been executed in the last 30 days. The move: run a competitive positioning session with Jason in early July to map each vendor's strengths/weaknesses, identify which competitor the EB is likely to favor (brand/relationship risk), and craft the Zycus wedge (AI-native, quantified ROI, agentic future) for the August workshops.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed: Alpla's procurement processes are fragmented, manual, and lack global visibility, preventing data-driven decision-making and creating operational inefficiencies. Jason Gordon articulated this on 27 March: 'We need a single platform that can handle our global operations' and emphasized that the current state requires manual workarounds, lacks AI/automation, and does not support strategic procurement goals. He stated that AI capabilities are critical to future-proof the investment ('AI is a big part of our evaluation criteria,' 27 March). The pain owner is Jason Gordon (Director of Procurement), who is leading the evaluation and has articulated the problem across calls. Inferred operational pain (not yet quantified): high cycle time for requisition-to-PO-to-payment, limited spend visibility across regions, manual invoice processing (10,000 invoices assumed per Next_Step 19 June OCR discussion), and inability to leverage AI for supplier negotiation or contract intelligence. The confirmed pain: fragmented global processes, lack of unified platform, manual workflows, no AI/automation. The gap: no quantified baseline (cost, time, error rate, FTE hours) to anchor the ROI case. The move: co-author the ROI model in July (before shortlist) to quantify the pain in Alpla's language and lock it to the relevant stakeholder differentiation.

The buyer is running a structured multi-vendor RFI/RFQ evaluation with five competing vendors (Coupa, GEP, iValua, JAGGAER, SAP Ariba per Competitors__c). decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are confirmed from RFI responses and buyer priorities articulated across calls. Jason Gordon stated on 27 March that AI capabilities are a primary evaluation criterion ('AI is a big part of our evaluation criteria') and that Alpla is assessing vendors on their ability to deliver a unified global S2P platform with strong AI/automation, ease of use, and implementation track record. The RFI response (completed by Zycus and submitted 30 March per Task) addressed Alpla's detailed requirements across modules (iSource, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iRequest, Merlin, Merlin Intake per Products__c). Jason emphasized on 27 March that Alpla's steering committee will evaluate ROI/business case rigor ('building a robust ROI/business case for the steering committee and board is mandatory') and that the final decision will weigh AI differentiation, cost, and implementation risk. The 19 June pricing call confirmed that invoice processing fees (OCR) are a decision variable, with Jason requesting a reprice to 48 cents per invoice if actual volume differs from the assumed 10,000 invoices (Next_Step 19 June). The confirmed criteria: (1) AI/automation capabilities (Merlin conversational UI, ANA supplier negotiation, contract intelligence), (2) unified global platform fit, (3) ROI/business case strength, (4) pricing competitiveness (especially OCR fees), (5) implementation track record. Inferred but not explicitly confirmed: compliance/audit trail, ERP integration depth, user adoption/change management support.
